questions.stem: A 35-year-old builder presents to ED with acute onset of low back pain after lifting a heavy load. He now has bilateral leg weakness, numbness in his perineum, and is unable to pass urine for 8 hours. On examination, there is reduced perianal sensation and a lax anal sphincter. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Lumbar disc prolapse without cauda equina involvement
questions.explanation: The combination of bilateral leg weakness, saddle anaesthesia, and urinary retention with reduced anal tone is classic for cauda equina syndrome, typically due to a large central disc herniation. Spinal cord compression would cause a sensory level and upper motor neuron signs; transverse myelitis evolves over hours to days; Guillain-Barré has ascending weakness and areflexia; and simple disc prolapse would not cause saddle or sphincter deficits.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman presents with a 2-week history of left leg pain and paraesthesia in the L5 distribution. She mentions occasional difficulty starting urination but denies incontinence. Symptoms are worse with sitting. On examination, left ankle dorsiflexion is 4/5, but perianal sensation is reduced on the left only and anal sphincter tone is normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Even unilateral perianal sensory loss and subtle urinary symptoms can indicate cauda equina syndrome, albeit an atypical presentation. The red flags are perianal sensory change and urinary hesitancy. An L5 radiculopathy would not affect perianal sensation; sciatic nerve palsy would spare the saddle area; spinal stenosis typically causes bilateral neurogenic claudication; peripheral neuropathy is diffuse and symmetric.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man who underwent an L4/L5 microdiscectomy 4 hours ago develops severe bilateral leg pain and numbness, and is unable to void despite a full bladder. He has reduced perianal sensation. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Cauda equina syndrome due to postoperative haematoma
questions.explanation: Postoperatively, a compressive haematoma in the spinal canal can cause acute cauda equina syndrome. The symptoms of bilateral leg pain, numbness, urinary retention, and perianal sensory loss are diagnostic. Spinal cord ischaemia would present with upper motor neuron signs; transverse myelitis evolves more slowly; meningitis would have fever and headache; retroperitoneal haematoma would cause thigh pain and hip flexion weakness, not perianal signs.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman presents with acute onset of bilateral leg weakness and numbness that has progressed over 2 days. She has a sensory level at T10, hyperreflexia in the legs, and extensor plantar responses. There is no perianal sensory loss and she can void normally. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A sensory level with hyperreflexia and extensor plantars indicates an upper motor neuron lesion above the conus medullaris, most commonly spinal cord compression (e.g., metastatic tumour). Cauda equina syndrome would have lower motor neuron signs and saddle anaesthesia. Guillain-Barré is ascending areflexic paralysis; multiple sclerosis usually relapsing-remitting; anterior spinal artery syndrome spares posterior columns.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and peripheral vascular disease complains of bilateral leg weakness and numbness for 3 months. He has fallen twice due to his legs 'giving way'. He wears incontinence pads for 'occasional dribbling' but attributes it to his prostate. On examination, there is mild bilateral leg weakness (4/5), absent ankle jerks, reduced perianal sensation, and a lax anal sphincter. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Despite the indolent course and comorbidities, the presence of perianal sensory loss and reduced anal tone indicate cauda equina syndrome, which can be chronic (e.g., from a slow-growing tumour or central disc prolapse). Diabetic neuropathy usually is distal symmetric, without sphincter involvement; spinal stenosis causes neurogenic claudication with symptom relief on sitting; PVD would cause claudication and absent pulses, not perianal signs; normal pressure hydrocephalus gives gait apraxia and cognitive decline.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with acute onset low back pain, bilateral sciatica, saddle anaesthesia, and inability to pass urine for 6 hours. She has no history of trauma. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: MRI of the whole spine is the gold standard for diagnosing cauda equina syndrome as it provides detailed visualisation of the cauda equina and identifies compressive lesions. X-ray and CT do not adequately show neural structures; CT myelogram is invasive and second-line; ultrasound is not relevant for cauda equina compression.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with chronic low back pain reports recent difficulty starting urination. Examination shows reduced perianal sensation but normal anal tone. A post-void bladder ultrasound is performed. Which residual volume threshold is most suggestive of cauda equina syndrome?
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines state that a post-void residual volume >200 mL is a red flag for cauda equina syndrome, indicating urinary retention. Lower thresholds are within normal range; higher volumes also suggest retention but 200 mL is the established diagnostic threshold.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man on warfarin presents with acute severe back pain and progressive bilateral leg weakness. He has a permanent pacemaker. Urgent MRI is contraindicated. Which investigation is most appropriate to assess for cauda equina compression?
questions.explanation: When MRI is contraindicated, CT myelogram is the recommended alternative to visualise the thecal sac and nerve roots for cauda equina compression. Plain CT, X-ray, ultrasound, and bone scan do not adequately assess neural compression.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman who underwent lumbar discectomy 6 weeks ago now presents with new bilateral leg pain, numbness in the saddle area, and loss of bladder sensation. What is the single most appropriate investigation?
questions.explanation: Urgent MRI of the lumbar spine is indicated to assess for postoperative haematoma, recurrent disc, or other compressive cause of cauda equina syndrome. CT and X-ray lack soft tissue detail; EMG/NCS are not for acute compression, and bone scan is irrelevant.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman at 32 weeks gestation presents with acute severe low back pain, bilateral sciatica, and inability to void. She has a history of lumbar disc prolapse. Which imaging investigation is most appropriate?
options: CT lumbar spine with abdominal shielding
No imaging – clinical diagnosis is sufficient
questions.explanation: MRI without gadolinium is safe in pregnancy and is the imaging of choice for suspected cauda equina syndrome; it avoids ionising radiation and provides excellent soft tissue detail. CT and X-ray involve radiation; ultrasound cannot visualise the canal; clinical diagnosis alone is insufficient given the urgency.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with acute-onset severe lower back pain, bilateral leg weakness, and urinary retention. MRI confirms a large central L4/L5 disc herniation compressing the cauda equina. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
questions.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome from disc herniation requires emergency surgical decompression to prevent permanent neurological damage; conservative measures and lumbar puncture are contraindicated and do not relieve compression.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman on warfarin for atrial fibrillation develops cauda equina syndrome due to a spontaneous spinal epidural haematoma (INR 3.4). Which of the following treatments is CONTRAINDICATED?
options: Emergency surgical decompression after INR reversal
questions.explanation: Intravenous thrombolysis is contraindicated as it would exacerbate bleeding; management requires reversal of anticoagulation with vitamin K and FFP followed by surgical evacuation.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with subacute bilateral leg paraesthesia and urinary hesitancy. MRI shows an enhancing lesion at the conus medullaris; subsequent biopsy confirms an ependymoma. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
questions.explanation: For spinal ependymoma causing cauda equina syndrome, the definitive treatment is surgical resection to decompress neural structures; corticosteroids are adjunctive but not curative.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with known Paget's disease of bone presents with acute lower back pain, bilateral leg weakness, and urinary retention. MRI shows spinal stenosis from L3 to S1 due to Pagetic bone overgrowth. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
questions.explanation: Acute cauda equina syndrome from Paget's disease requires urgent surgical decompression; medical therapy (bisphosphonates) treats the underlying bone disease but does not relieve acute neural compression.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus presents with bilateral leg weakness and urinary retention. MRI shows no compressive lesion but thickened, enhancing nerve roots. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
questions.explanation: In SLE, cauda equina syndrome due to vasculitic neuropathy is treated with high-dose corticosteroids as first-line immunosuppression; surgery is not indicated as there is no structural compression.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with acute low back pain and right leg pain. She reports numbness in the saddle area and has not passed urine for 8 hours. On examination, perianal sensation is reduced and anal sphincter tone is decreased. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Request a CT scan of the lumbosacral spine
Arrange emergency MRI of the lumbar spine
Refer to physiotherapy for pelvic floor exercises
questions.explanation: Urgent MRI is the priority in suspected cauda equina syndrome to confirm compression and guide surgical decompression. Catheterisation may be needed later but imaging should not be delayed.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with chronic low back pain develops new left leg weakness and numbness in the saddle area over 2 days. He has no urinary symptoms and digital rectal examination reveals normal anal tone and voluntary contraction. MRI shows a large L4/L5 disc prolapse compressing the cauda equina. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer for lumbar epidural steroid injection
questions.explanation: Even without urinary involvement, cauda equina syndrome with motor deficits warrants urgent surgical decompression (within 24-48 hours) to prevent permanent neurological damage. Steroids are not indicated.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman presents with acute severe low back pain and bilateral leg numbness. She is incontinent of urine and has perianal anaesthesia. On examination, she has no voluntary anal contraction. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange emergency MRI of the lumbar spine
questions.explanation: In the setting of incontinence and perianal anaesthesia, cauda equina syndrome is likely. The priority is to obtain an emergency MRI to identify the cause (e.g., disc prolapse, haematoma) for urgent surgical referral.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man underwent emergency lumbar decompression for cauda equina syndrome 12 hours ago. He now complains of a severe headache worse on sitting up and photophobia. There is no fever or neck stiffness. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Obtain a blood sample for culture
Lie the patient flat and administer intravenous fluids
questions.explanation: Post-dural headache from a CSF leak is likely. Immediate management includes lying flat and increasing fluid intake to promote dural healing. Further interventions (e.g., blood patch) may be considered if conservative measures fail.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents with acute severe back pain and bilateral leg weakness over 4 hours. She has perianal numbness but no urinary incontinence. INR is 4.5. MRI shows a large epidural haematoma compressing the cauda equina. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Order a repeat INR in 6 hours
Administer vitamin K and prothrombin complex concentrate
Apply a lumbar epidural blood patch
questions.explanation: The haematoma is caused by anticoagulation. Before surgery, anticoagulation must be reversed urgently to minimise bleeding risk. Vitamin K and prothrombin complex concentrate are the reversal agents of choice.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute low back pain after a fall. She reports a sensation of numbness when wiping after defecation. On examination, she has mild weakness of plantarflexion bilaterally. Which single clinical finding most strongly supports a diagnosis of cauda equina syndrome?
questions.explanation: Perianal sensation loss (saddle anesthesia) is the hallmark early finding of cauda equina syndrome, often preceding motor deficits. While urinary retention and anal tone loss are also key, perianal numbness is the most specific early red flag.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a history of lumbar disc disease presents with a three-day history of worsening back pain and difficulty initiating urination. His bladder scan shows a post-void residual of 250 ml. He has no perianal numbness and normal anal tone. Which single finding, if present, would most clearly meet the criteria for emergency MRI in suspected cauda equina syndrome?
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines state that acute urinary retention with post-void residual >200 ml is a red flag for cauda equina syndrome and warrants urgent imaging, even without other classic signs like saddle anesthesia.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman presents with progressive leg weakness and difficulty walking. On examination, she has brisk knee jerks, upgoing plantars, and a sensory level at T10. She reports no perianal numbness. Which finding, if present, would point to cauda equina syndrome rather than myelopathy?
questions.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome involves lower motor neuron signs, so perianal sensation loss (saddle anesthesia) is characteristic, whereas hyperreflexia, Babinski, and spasticity are upper motor neuron signs of myelopathy.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known cauda equina syndrome from a large disc extrusion has been in hospital for 12 hours. He initially had saddle anesthesia and mild urinary hesitancy. Now he has developed complete urinary retention. Which finding indicates that he has crossed the threshold for irreversible bladder dysfunction?
questions.explanation: Complete (painless) urinary retention is a late finding indicating severe compression and high risk of permanent bladder dysfunction, requiring immediate decompression. Saddle anesthesia alone can be present earlier.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents with sudden onset of severe interscapular pain followed within 30 minutes by bilateral leg weakness and perianal numbness. Which feature of the history most strongly suggests spinal epidural hematoma as the cause of her cauda equina syndrome?
questions.explanation: While anticoagulation is a risk factor, the hallmark of spinal epidural hematoma is acute onset over minutes to hours, distinguishing it from compressive lesions like disc prolapse which develop over hours to days. The combination of rapid onset with back pain and neurological deficits is classic.
